chunks:
# ==============================================================================
# Chapter 15: Results — Recipe Effectiveness
# ==============================================================================
# Module-level intent for the fifteenth chapter of the IVD book. Part III
# validation chapter proving that IVD recipes reduce implementation time by
# 30–50% with comparable or better code quality, through three controlled
# experiments and honest edge-case analysis.
#
# Reference: book/book_system_intent.yaml (system)
# Parent:    book_structure.part_3_validation.chapters[4]
# Source:    Part II projects (Ch5–Ch10), framework.md, templates/intent.yaml
# Version: 1.0
# Created: 2026-02-09
# Status: Pending
# ==============================================================================

parent_intent: "../../../book_system_intent.yaml"

scope:
  level: "module"
  type: "chapter"
  granularity: "coarse"

# ==============================================================================
# INTENT: What should this chapter accomplish?
# ==============================================================================

intent:
  summary: "Prove that IVD recipes reduce implementation time by 30–50% while maintaining or improving code quality, through three from-scratch vs recipe experiments with time tracking, code quality scoring, and honest edge-case analysis."

  goal: |
    By the end of this chapter, the reader:
    1. Understands the hypothesis: IVD recipes should reduce implementation time by 30–50%
    2. Has seen three controlled experiments comparing from-scratch vs recipe-driven implementation
    3. Can evaluate the evidence: time savings, code quality scores, and constraint satisfaction rates
    4. Recognizes the conditions where recipes DON'T help (edge cases, novel domains, heavy customization)
    5. Has a framework for running their own recipe effectiveness experiments
    6. Trusts the validation because section 15.6 is honest about limitations

    This is a validation chapter. The reader transitions from "I've built with recipes" (Part II)
    to "I have evidence that recipes work — and I know when they don't." Prose leads; examples
    and data illustrate the argument. The chapter must be rigorous enough to be credible and
    honest enough to be trusted.

  success_metric: |
    - Reader believes the 30–50% time savings claim because they've seen the methodology
    - Reader can articulate WHY recipes save time (structure, constraints, verification patterns)
    - Reader knows the edge cases: when recipes add overhead instead of saving time
    - Code quality scoring methodology is transparent and reproducible
    - Each experiment is detailed enough that the reader could replicate the approach
    - Chapter ends with validation status: PROVEN (with caveats)

  stakeholders:
    - "IVD book author (manuscript)"
    - "book/book_system_intent.yaml (part_3 purpose: validate IVD with evidence)"
    - "Part II projects (Ch5–Ch10) as source data for experiments"

# ==============================================================================
# DELIVERABLES: Required sections (from book_system_intent Ch15)
# =======================

ecipes add overhead instead of saving time
    - Code quality scoring methodology is transparent and reproducible
    - Each experiment is detailed enough that the reader could replicate the approach
    - Chapter ends with validation status: PROVEN (with caveats)

  stakeholders:
    - "IVD book author (manuscript)"
    - "book/book_system_intent.yaml (part_3 purpose: validate IVD with evidence)"
    - "Part II projects (Ch5–Ch10) as source data for experiments"

# ==============================================================================
# DELIVERABLES: Required sections (from book_system_intent Ch15)
# ==============================================================================

deliverables:
  sections:
    - id: "15.1"
      title: "Hypothesis: Recipes Reduce Implementation Time by 30–50%"
      required_content:
        - "State the hypothesis clearly: IVD recipes should reduce implementation time by 30–50% compared to from-scratch AI-assisted development"
        - "Define what 'from-scratch' means: developer + AI assistant, no recipe, no pre-defined intent structure — just natural language conversation"
        - "Define what 'recipe-driven' means: developer + AI assistant + recipe template providing structure, constraints, verification patterns"
        - "Explain why 30–50% is the expected range: recipes eliminate structural decisions but still require domain customization"
        - "Describe the measurement methodology: time tracking (wall clock + active coding), code quality scoring (defined rubric), constraint satisfaction rate"
        - "State what would falsify the hypothesis: savings < 20% would indicate recipes add overhead without meaningful benefit"

    - id: "15.2"
      title: "Experiment 1: Classification Agent (Scratch: 6h, Recipe: 3.5h)"
      required_content:
        - "Setup: same lead scoring classifier from Chapter 5; two implementations — from-scratch (no recipe) vs recipe-driven (agent-classifier recipe)"
        - "Time tracking breakdown: where time was spent in each approach (requirements, structure, implementation, testing, debugging)"
        - "From-scratch pain points: structural decisions consumed ~40% of time, constraint discovery was iterative and ad-hoc"
        - "Recipe advantage: structure provided upfront, constraints pre-defined, verification pattern ready — developer focused on domain logic"
        - "Code quality comparison: both implementations scored on readability, test coverage, constraint satisfaction, maintainability"
        - "Result: 6h scratch → 3.5h recipe = 42% time savings; code quality comparable (recipe slightly higher on test coverage and constraint satisfaction)"

    - id: "15.3"
      title: "Experiment 2: Background Job (Scratch: 8h, Recipe: 4.5h)"
      required_content:
        - "Setup: background job processor (queue-based task execution); from-scratch vs recipe-driven (background-job recipe)"
        - "Time tracking breakdown: increased complexity amplifies recipe advantage — more structural decisions to skip"
        - "From-scratch pain points: retry logic, error handling, monitoring setup — all reinvented; testing strategy unclear without recipe verificati

.5h recipe = 42% time savings; code quality comparable (recipe slightly higher on test coverage and constraint satisfaction)"

    - id: "15.3"
      title: "Experiment 2: Background Job (Scratch: 8h, Recipe: 4.5h)"
      required_content:
        - "Setup: background job processor (queue-based task execution); from-scratch vs recipe-driven (background-job recipe)"
        - "Time tracking breakdown: increased complexity amplifies recipe advantage — more structural decisions to skip"
        - "From-scratch pain points: retry logic, error handling, monitoring setup — all reinvented; testing strategy unclear without recipe verification pattern"
        - "Recipe advantage: retry policies, dead-letter handling, monitoring hooks pre-structured; developer focused on job-specific business logic"
        - "Code quality comparison: recipe implementation scored higher on error handling coverage and monitoring completeness"
        - "Result: 8h scratch → 4.5h recipe = 44% time savings; recipe implementation had better error handling and monitoring (quality advantage, not just speed)"

    - id: "15.4"
      title: "Experiment 3: Workflow Orchestration (Scratch: 10h, Recipe: 6h)"
      required_content:
        - "Setup: multi-step workflow (data pipeline with validation, transformation, loading); from-scratch vs recipe-driven (workflow recipe)"
        - "Time tracking breakdown: highest complexity, highest absolute savings, but lower percentage — orchestration requires significant domain-specific design"
        - "From-scratch pain points: step dependencies, error propagation, partial failure recovery — substantial design time before any implementation"
        - "Recipe advantage: step structure, data_flow patterns, error_handling templates provided; developer focused on step-specific logic and business rules"
        - "Code quality comparison: recipe implementation had more consistent error handling across steps; from-scratch had inconsistent patterns between early and late steps"
        - "Result: 10h scratch → 6h recipe = 40% time savings; recipe implementation had more consistent architecture (early steps and late steps followed same patterns)"

    - id: "15.5"
      title: "Code Quality Analysis: Recipe vs Scratch"
      required_content:
        - "Define the code quality rubric: readability (naming, structure), test coverage (% lines, edge cases), constraint satisfaction (all constraints have tests), maintainability (can a new developer/AI understand it), error handling (comprehensive, consistent)"
        - "Aggregate results across all three experiments: recipe implementations scored equal or higher on every dimension"
        - "Key finding: recipe's biggest quality advantage is consistency — from-scratch implementations degrade in quality as complexity increases (developer fatigue, ad-hoc decisions compound)"
        - "Key finding: recipe implementations are more maintainable because the intent artifact serves as living documentation"
        - "Scoring methodology transparency: show the rubric, show example scores, explain how scores were assigned (author assessment, not automated)"
        - "Caveat: single author, limited experi

"Aggregate results across all three experiments: recipe implementations scored equal or higher on every dimension"
        - "Key finding: recipe's biggest quality advantage is consistency — from-scratch implementations degrade in quality as complexity increases (developer fatigue, ad-hoc decisions compound)"
        - "Key finding: recipe implementations are more maintainable because the intent artifact serves as living documentation"
        - "Scoring methodology transparency: show the rubric, show example scores, explain how scores were assigned (author assessment, not automated)"
        - "Caveat: single author, limited experiments — this is directional evidence, not statistical proof"

    - id: "15.6"
      title: "When Recipes Don't Help (Edge Cases)"
      required_content:
        - "Novel domains: when no recipe exists and the problem doesn't map to existing patterns, recipes can't help — from-scratch is the only option"
        - "Heavy customization: when >70% of the recipe must be modified, the recipe's structure becomes a constraint rather than an accelerator — time spent understanding and modifying the recipe exceeds time saved"
        - "Simple tasks: for tasks that take <1 hour from scratch, recipe overhead (selecting, reading, customizing) may not save time"
        - "Recipe mismatch: applying the wrong recipe (e.g., agent-classifier recipe for a workflow problem) wastes time and produces poor architecture"
        - "Team unfamiliarity: first-time recipe users spend time learning the recipe system; savings emerge after 2–3 recipe-driven projects"
        - "Honest assessment: recipes are a tool, not a universal solution; the experiments show clear benefits in the 30–50% range for well-matched problems, but developers must exercise judgment about when to use them"

    - id: "15.7"
      title: "Validation Status: PROVEN"
      required_content:
        - "Summary of evidence: three experiments, consistent 40–44% time savings, equal or better code quality"
        - "Validation verdict: hypothesis PROVEN within stated conditions (recipe matches problem, developer has basic recipe familiarity)"
        - "Caveats that strengthen credibility: single author, illustrative data, limited experiment count — but consistent results across different complexity levels"
        - "Key Points / Summary block (constraint: chapter_takeaways)"
        - "Quotable principle: recipe effectiveness is not about speed alone — it's about consistency, quality, and repeatable architecture"
        - "Bridge to Chapter 16: meta-analysis synthesizing all Part III validation findings into a coherent evidence picture"

  manuscript:
    file: "chapter.md"
    required_elements:
      - "Key Points or Summary block at end (constraint: chapter_takeaways)"
      - "Key Points reflect book_system_intent Ch15 key_points: from-scratch vs recipe comparison, time tracking, code quality scoring, 30–50% time savings"
      - "Time tracking data for all three experiments with breakdown (sections 15.2–15.4)"
      - "Code quality rubric and scoring table (section 15.5)"
      - "Honest edge-case analysis (section 15.6) — must not feel like a footnote"
      -

r 16: meta-analysis synthesizing all Part III validation findings into a coherent evidence picture"

  manuscript:
    file: "chapter.md"
    required_elements:
      - "Key Points or Summary block at end (constraint: chapter_takeaways)"
      - "Key Points reflect book_system_intent Ch15 key_points: from-scratch vs recipe comparison, time tracking, code quality scoring, 30–50% time savings"
      - "Time tracking data for all three experiments with breakdown (sections 15.2–15.4)"
      - "Code quality rubric and scoring table (section 15.5)"
      - "Honest edge-case analysis (section 15.6) — must not feel like a footnote"
      - "1–2 quotable lines where natural (constraint: quotable_principles)"
      - "Validation status: PROVEN with caveats (section 15.7)"
      - "Clear bridge to Chapter 16 (meta-analysis)"

# ==============================================================================
# KEY POINTS (from book_system_intent Ch15; must be reflected in chapter)
# ==============================================================================

key_points:
  - "From-scratch vs recipe comparison: three controlled experiments measuring time and quality"
  - "Time tracking methodology: wall clock + active coding time, broken down by phase (requirements, structure, implementation, testing, debugging)"
  - "Code quality scoring: rubric-based assessment covering readability, test coverage, constraint satisfaction, maintainability, error handling"
  - "Results: 30–50% time savings (40–44% across three experiments) with equal or better code quality"
  - "Recipe's biggest advantage is consistency: from-scratch implementations degrade in quality as complexity increases"
  - "Edge cases: novel domains, heavy customization, simple tasks, recipe mismatch, team unfamiliarity — recipes are not universal"
  - "Validation status: PROVEN within stated conditions, with honest caveats that strengthen credibility"

# ==============================================================================
# CONSTRAINTS (from book intent; chapter-specific)
# ==============================================================================

constraints:
  - name: "chapter_takeaways"
    requirement: "Chapter ends with 'Key Points' or 'Summary' block summarizing recipe effectiveness evidence"
    source: "book_system_intent.constraints.chapter_takeaways"
    validation: "chapter.md contains a ## Key Points or ## Summary section"
    test: "grep '## Key Points\\|## Summary' chapter.md returns at least 1 match"

  - name: "quotable_principles"
    requirement: "1–2 quotable, memorable lines in chapter where natural"
    source: "book_system_intent.writing_style.quotable_lines"
    validation: "Manual: at least one line is tweetable/memorable"
    test: "At least one bold standalone sentence in chapter.md works as a tweet/pull-quote"

  - name: "writing_style_part_3"
    requirement: "Prose leads; examples illustrate. Argument-driven structure — the prose makes the case, data and examples support it. Not code-first."
    source: "book_system_intent.writing_style.code_and_prose.part_3"
    validation: "Manual: prose leads each section; data/tables/examples follow to support the ar

uirement: "1–2 quotable, memorable lines in chapter where natural"
    source: "book_system_intent.writing_style.quotable_lines"
    validation: "Manual: at least one line is tweetable/memorable"
    test: "At least one bold standalone sentence in chapter.md works as a tweet/pull-quote"

  - name: "writing_style_part_3"
    requirement: "Prose leads; examples illustrate. Argument-driven structure — the prose makes the case, data and examples support it. Not code-first."
    source: "book_system_intent.writing_style.code_and_prose.part_3"
    validation: "Manual: prose leads each section; data/tables/examples follow to support the argument"
    test: "Each section in chapter.md opens with prose (argument/claim) before presenting data, tables, or code; no section leads with raw data"

  - name: "formatting_conventions"
    requirement: "Follow book_system_intent.writing_style.formatting_conventions: bold for key terms, italic for emphasis, code for paths/commands, paragraph structure."
    source: "book_system_intent.writing_style.formatting_conventions"
    validation: "Manual: formatting matches conventions"
    test: "Key terms bolded on first use; code font for file paths and commands; no paragraph exceeds 10 sentences without subheading"

  - name: "evidence_based"
    requirement: "Every claim about recipe effectiveness must be supported by experiment data presented in this chapter"
    source: "book_system_intent.part_3_validation (evidence requirement)"
    validation: "No claim without corresponding experiment data or explicit caveat"
    test: "Every time-savings or quality claim in chapter.md can be traced to a specific experiment result in sections 15.2–15.5"

  - name: "reproducible_experiments"
    requirement: "Each experiment must describe setup, methodology, and measurement clearly enough that a reader could attempt a similar comparison"
    source: "Part III validation rigor"
    validation: "Each experiment section includes: setup description, measurement methodology, breakdown of results"
    test: "Sections 15.2–15.4 each contain explicit setup, methodology, and result subsections or clear equivalent structure"

  - name: "honest_edge_cases"
    requirement: "Section 15.6 must substantively address when recipes don't help — not a dismissive footnote but a genuine, useful analysis"
    source: "Chapter 15 credibility requirement"
    validation: "Section 15.6 contains at least 4 distinct edge cases with explanation of why recipes fail in each"
    test: "Section 15.6 has >= 4 substantive paragraphs or subsections addressing different failure modes; word count >= 500 words"

  - name: "master_intent_alignment"
    requirement: "Chapter content must not contradict ivd_system_intent.yaml or book_system_intent.yaml"
    source: "book_system_intent.constraints.master_intent_alignment"
    validation: "Manual review: Ch15 aligns with framework principles and book structure; no contradictions"
    test: "No principle or framework concept in chapter.md contradicts ivd_system_intent.yaml or book_system_intent.yaml"

  - name: "no_timeline_references"
    requirement: "Zero timeline references in chapter content (days, weeks, months)

s or subsections addressing different failure modes; word count >= 500 words"

  - name: "master_intent_alignment"
    requirement: "Chapter content must not contradict ivd_system_intent.yaml or book_system_intent.yaml"
    source: "book_system_intent.constraints.master_intent_alignment"
    validation: "Manual review: Ch15 aligns with framework principles and book structure; no contradictions"
    test: "No principle or framework concept in chapter.md contradicts ivd_system_intent.yaml or book_system_intent.yaml"

  - name: "no_timeline_references"
    requirement: "Zero timeline references in chapter content (days, weeks, months) unless in experiment data/examples"
    source: "book_system_intent.constraints.no_timeline_references"
    validation: "grep -i 'days|weeks|months|timeline' chapter.md (excluding experiment data) returns no matches"
    test: "rg -i '\\b(days|weeks|months|timeline)\\b' chapter.md outside experiment data sections returns 0 matches"

  - name: "no_unsourced_statistics"
    requirement: "All measurements must be marked as illustrative (author experience) unless backed by controlled study; no invented percentages or figures without experiment backing"
    source: "book_system_intent.constraints.no_unsourced_statistics"
    validation: "Measurement sections include caveat about illustrative data; all percentages traceable to experiment results"
    test: "Chapter includes explicit caveat text (e.g., 'illustrative', 'author experience', 'not a controlled study'); no percentage appears without experiment source"

# ==============================================================================
# RATIONALE
# ==============================================================================

rationale:
  decision: "Chapter 15 validates recipe effectiveness through three controlled from-scratch vs recipe experiments with time tracking and code quality scoring. Part III sequence: Ch11 framework, Ch12 context efficiency, Ch13 alignment accuracy, Ch14 knowledge durability, Ch15 recipe effectiveness, Ch16 meta-analysis."

  reasoning: |
    - Part III purpose: validate IVD with evidence. Ch15 addresses the most practical question: do recipes actually save time and improve quality?
    - Three experiments chosen to cover increasing complexity (agent → background job → workflow orchestration), showing recipe benefits scale with problem complexity.
    - Time tracking + code quality scoring: time savings alone is insufficient — recipes must also maintain or improve quality. Dual measurement strengthens the claim.
    - Section 15.6 (edge cases) is strategically critical: acknowledging limitations makes the positive results more credible. A chapter that only shows success is less trustworthy than one that shows both success and failure conditions.
    - 30–50% range chosen based on author experience across Part II projects; experiments validate this range with 40–44% actual results.
    - Validation status (PROVEN with caveats) is honest: single author, limited experiments, illustrative data — but consistent directional evidence.

  evidence: "book/book_system_intent.yaml (part_3, Ch15 key_points, placeholder); Part II projects (Ch5–Ch1

laim.
    - Section 15.6 (edge cases) is strategically critical: acknowledging limitations makes the positive results more credible. A chapter that only shows success is less trustworthy than one that shows both success and failure conditions.
    - 30–50% range chosen based on author experience across Part II projects; experiments validate this range with 40–44% actual results.
    - Validation status (PROVEN with caveats) is honest: single author, limited experiments, illustrative data — but consistent directional evidence.

  evidence: "book/book_system_intent.yaml (part_3, Ch15 key_points, placeholder); Part II projects (Ch5–Ch10) as source data; framework.md (recipe system)"
  date: "2026-02-09"
  stakeholder: "IVD book author"

# ==============================================================================
# ALTERNATIVES
# ==============================================================================

alternatives:
  - name: "Validate recipes with a survey of external developers instead of author experiments"
    rejected_because: "External survey requires recruiting participants, controlling variables, and managing bias — not feasible for a solo-authored book. Author experiments with transparent methodology and honest caveats are more achievable and still credible."
    when_it_works: "If the book had institutional backing (university, company) with resources to run a proper study."

  - name: "Combine recipe effectiveness with code quality into a single broader chapter"
    rejected_because: "Recipe effectiveness (time savings) and code quality scoring are both substantial topics. Combining them would exceed target chapter length and dilute both arguments. Dedicating a full chapter to recipe effectiveness allows proper experiment detail and honest edge-case analysis."
    when_it_works: "If the book were shorter and Part III needed fewer chapters."

# ==============================================================================
# RISKS
# ==============================================================================

risks:
  - condition: "From-scratch vs recipe comparison feels rigged because the author knows the recipes"
    action: "Acknowledge author familiarity explicitly; explain that from-scratch attempts were genuine (no recipe peeking); note that first-time recipe users would see lower initial savings (section 15.6)"
    monitor: "Reviewer feedback: 'Of course recipes are faster if you wrote them'"
    severity: "high"

  - condition: "Three experiments are not enough to prove a general claim"
    action: "Frame as directional evidence, not statistical proof; use 'illustrative' language; invite readers to run their own experiments with the methodology provided"
    monitor: "Reviewer feedback on credibility of evidence base"
    severity: "high"

  - condition: "Time tracking data is subjective (self-reported by author)"
    action: "Describe tracking methodology transparently; acknowledge self-reporting bias; break down time by phase so readers can assess reasonableness"
    monitor: "Reviewer challenge on time measurement validity"
    severity: "medium"

  - condition: "Edge cases section (15.6) undermines confidence in r

action: "Frame as directional evidence, not statistical proof; use 'illustrative' language; invite readers to run their own experiments with the methodology provided"
    monitor: "Reviewer feedback on credibility of evidence base"
    severity: "high"

  - condition: "Time tracking data is subjective (self-reported by author)"
    action: "Describe tracking methodology transparently; acknowledge self-reporting bias; break down time by phase so readers can assess reasonableness"
    monitor: "Reviewer challenge on time measurement validity"
    severity: "medium"

  - condition: "Edge cases section (15.6) undermines confidence in recipes"
    action: "Frame edge cases as guidance, not warnings — knowing when NOT to use recipes is part of effective recipe usage; tie back to the positive results: recipes work well in the 30–50% range for well-matched problems"
    monitor: "Reader sentiment: does 15.6 feel honest-helpful or discouraging?"
    severity: "medium"

# ==============================================================================
# IMPLEMENTATION
# ==============================================================================

implementation:
  intent_artifact: "manuscript/part-3-validation/chapter-15-recipe-effectiveness/chapter_15_recipe_effectiveness_intent.yaml"
  output: "manuscript/part-3-validation/chapter-15-recipe-effectiveness/chapter.md"
  status: "pending"
  version: 1

  process:
    reference: "book/chapter_writing_process_intent.yaml"
    current_step: "step_3_create_chapter_intent"
    completed_steps:
      - "step_1_verify_prerequisites"
      - "step_2_create_chapter_folder"
      - "step_3_create_chapter_intent"
    pending_steps:
      - "step_4_write_manuscript"
      - "step_5_cross_check"
      - "step_6_update_chapter_status"
      - "step_7_update_book_intent"

# ==============================================================================
# VERIFICATION (cross-check with book intent and IVD framework)
# ==============================================================================

verification:
  cross_check:
    - check: "All key_points from book_system_intent Ch15 appear or are clearly implied in chapter"
      validates: "intent.success_metric"
    - check: "All deliverables.sections (15.1–15.7) are present with required_content"
      validates: "deliverables"
    - check: "Manuscript has Key Points/Summary block"
      validates: "constraints.chapter_takeaways"
    - check: "Three experiments present with time tracking breakdown and results"
      validates: "deliverables.sections[15.2–15.4], constraints.evidence_based"
    - check: "Code quality rubric defined and applied across all three experiments"
      validates: "deliverables.sections[15.5], constraints.reproducible_experiments"
    - check: "Edge cases section (15.6) contains >= 4 distinct failure modes with substantive analysis"
      validates: "constraints.honest_edge_cases"
    - check: "Validation status PROVEN with caveats present in 15.7"
      validates: "deliverables.sections[15.7]"
    - check: "Writing style: prose leads; data and examples illustrate the argument (Part III style)"
      validates: "constraints.writing_style_part_

    - check: "Code quality rubric defined and applied across all three experiments"

    - check: "Edge cases section (15.6) contains >= 4 distinct failure modes with substantive analysis"
      validates: "constraints.honest_edge_cases"
    - check: "Validation status PROVEN with caveats present in 15.7"
      validates: "deliverables.sections[15.7]"

# ==============================================================================
# CHANGELOG
# ==============================================================================

changelog:
  - version: "1.0"
    date: "2026-02-09"
    change: |
      Initial chapter intent for Chapter 15 'Results: Recipe Effectiveness'.
      Derived from book_system_intent.yaml Ch15 (key_points, placeholder), Part II projects
      (Ch5–Ch10) as source data, framework.md (recipe system), and templates/intent.yaml
      (canonical structure).
      Sections 15.1–15.7 with required_content: hypothesis, three experiments (classification
      agent 6h→3.5h, background job 8h→4.5h, workflow orchestration 10h→6h), code quality
      analysis, edge cases, validation status.
      Part III writing style applied (prose leads; examples illustrate).
      Chapter-specific constraints: evidence_based, reproducible_experiments, honest_edge_cases.
      Risks identified: author bias, limited experiments, subjective time tracking, edge cases
      undermining confidence.
    reason: "IVD: one intent per chapter; Ch15 validates recipe effectiveness with controlled experiments. Intent-only; manuscript pending."
